title: Everyday conversations — phone calls & messages
description: Learn to make and receive phone calls, leave voicemails, and write simple text messages in English.

content.explanation: These are essential phrases for making and receiving phone calls in English.

phrases.phrase: Could you speak more slowly, please?
I'm sorry, I didn't catch that.
phrases.note: Polite way to ask to repeat
Polite way to end a call

content.explanation: In text messages, English is often shorter and more casual. Learn these common patterns.

tasks.title: Could for polite requests on the phone

content.explanation: On the phone, 'could' is used for almost all polite requests.

examples.sentence: Could I speak to Mr Sharma, please?
Could you hold on a moment?
Could you give him a message?
examples.note: Asking someone to pass a message

examples.sentence: Could you call me back later?
Could you spell that for me?
examples.note: When you need to clarify spelling

content.structure: Could + you + base verb + please? — always polite, always appropriate

content.instruction: Repeat clearly and naturally. Phone conversations need clear pronunciation.

phrases.text: Hello, this is Priya speaking. Could I speak to Mr Rajan?
Could you hold on a moment, please?
I'm sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat it?
I'll call you back in ten minutes.
Thank you for calling. Have a good day!

content.question: You call an office to speak to your manager. How do you start the call?
options: Hello, this is Arjun speaking. Could I speak to Ms Divya, please?
content.explanation: Introduce yourself first, then politely ask for the person using 'Could I speak to'. ✓

content.question: The person on the phone is speaking very fast and you can't understand. What do you say?
options: I'm sorry, could you speak more slowly, please?
content.explanation: Polite and professional: I'm sorry + could you + speak more slowly + please. ✓

content.question: Your friend texts: 'Running a bit late. Be there in 20.' What does this mean?
options: They will arrive about 20 minutes late.
content.explanation: 'Running late' = arriving late. 'Be there in 20' = will arrive in 20 minutes.

content.question: '___ you hold on a moment? I'll check for you.'
content.explanation: Could + you + base verb = polite phone request. Could you hold on? ✓

content.sentence: Hello, ___ is Meera speaking. Could I ___ to Dr Suresh, please? I'm ___ I didn't catch his number — could you ___ it again?
content.explanation: this is [name] ✓, speak to ✓, I'm sorry ✓ (apology before request), repeat that ✓.

content.sentence: A texts: 'Are you free at 6?' B replies: '___ good! See you ___.' Later B texts: 'Running a bit ___. Be there in 15. ___ me know if that's okay.'
content.explanation: Sounds good ✓, See you then ✓, Running late ✓, Let me know ✓.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct polite phone request.
content.correct_sentence: Could you leave a message for him?
content.explanation: Could + you + leave + a message + for + him?

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct phone call opening.
content.explanation: Hello + this is + [name] + speaking = standard formal phone opening.

content.instruction: Match each phone situation to the correct phrase.

pairs.prompt: You need the other person to wait.
pairs.answer: Could you hold on a moment?

pairs.prompt: You didn't understand what was said.
pairs.answer: I'm sorry, I didn't catch that.

pairs.prompt: You want to end the call politely.
pairs.answer: I'll let you go now. Take care!

pairs.prompt: You are calling someone and need to introduce yourself.

tasks.title: Dialogue — A phone call at work

content.context: Ananya calls her colleague's office to leave a message.

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.line: Good afternoon, Apex Solutions. How can I help you?

dialogue.speaker: Ananya
dialogue.line: Good afternoon. This is Ananya Krishnan calling. Could I speak to Mr Vikram Nair, please?

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.line: I'm afraid Mr Nair is in a meeting at the moment. Could I take a message?

dialogue.speaker: Ananya
dialogue.line: Yes, please. Could you tell him that I called regarding the project proposal?

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.line: Of course. Could you spell your last name for me, please?

dialogue.speaker: Ananya
dialogue.line: Sure — it's K-R-I-S-H-N-A-N.

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.line: Thank you. And your contact number?

dialogue.speaker: Ananya
dialogue.line: It's 9845 001 234. Could you ask him to call me back before 5pm?

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.line: Certainly, Ms Krishnan. I'll pass on the message.

dialogue.speaker: Ananya
dialogue.line: Thank you so much. Goodbye!

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.line: Goodbye! Have a good day.

comprehension_questions.question: Why can't Ananya speak to Mr Nair?
options: He is busy on another call
He has left for the day

comprehension_questions.question: When does Ananya want Mr Nair to call back?

content.instruction: Which sentence is NOT natural for a professional phone call?
content.wrong_sentence: Oi! Is Sharma there? Tell him Raj called.
options: Oi! Is Sharma there? Tell him Raj called.
Hello, this is Raj speaking. Could I speak to Mr Sharma?
Good morning. This is Raj calling. Is Mr Sharma available?
Hello, could I leave a message for Mr Sharma please?
content.explanation: 'Oi!' is very rude/informal for a professional call. Always use Hello + introduce yourself + Could I speak to. ✓

content.scenario: You are at work. A colleague's phone rings but they are away from their desk. You answer it.
content.question: What is the most appropriate thing to say?
options: Good morning, Ravi's phone. This is Meena speaking. He's away from his desk — can I take a message?
content.explanation: Identify whose phone it is + your name + explain the situation + offer to help. Professional and complete. ✓

content.instruction: Speak for 60 seconds. Role play a complete phone call. You are calling a doctor's clinic to book an appointment. Cover: greeting, reason for call, requesting an appointment, confirming details.
content.prompt: Call Dr Sharma's clinic. You need to book an appointment for next week.
content.example_response: Good morning. This is Arun Kumar calling. Could I speak to the receptionist, please? Good morning — I'd like to book an appointment with Dr Sharma if possible. I've been having a bad headache for three days. Could I come in sometime next week? Tuesday morning would be perfect. Could you tell me what time the clinic opens? Nine o'clock — great. Could I book a slot at 9:30 then? Wonderful. My name is Arun Kumar — A-R-U-N K-U-M-A-R. My number is 9845 112 233. Could you send me a confirmation message? Thank you so much. Goodbye!

content.instruction: Speak for 45 seconds. Read aloud a text message conversation between two friends making plans, then explain what they agreed.
content.prompt: Create and read aloud a text exchange: two friends planning to meet for dinner tonight.
content.example_response: Here is a text exchange. Friend 1: Hey! Are you free tonight? Friend 2: Yes! What's the plan? Friend 1: Want to try that new restaurant near the beach? Friend 2: Sounds amazing! What time? Friend 1: 7:30? Friend 2: Perfect. See you then! Friend 1: Running a bit late — be there by 7:45. Friend 2: No worries. Let me know when you're outside. So in this exchange, the two friends made plans to meet at a new restaurant near the beach at around 7:45pm. Friend 2 will wait for a message when Friend 1 arrives.

content.sentence: A: Hello, ___ is Sara. Could I ___ to Mr Patel? B: I'm ___, he's not available right now. ___ I take a message? A: Yes please — could you ___ him I called about the invoice? B: Of course. I'll ___ on the message. A: Thank you so much. ___!
content.explanation: This is ✓, speak to ✓, I'm afraid ✓ (polite way to say no), Could I ✓, tell him ✓, pass on ✓, Goodbye ✓.

what_you_learned: Starting calls: Hello, this is ___ speaking. / Could I speak to ___?
During calls: Could you hold on? / Could you repeat that? / I didn't catch that.
Ending calls: I'll let you go. / I'll call you back. / Thank you for calling.
Could + you + base verb = all polite phone requests
Text message language: Sounds good! / Running late / Be there in 10 / Let me know
content.next_lesson_title: Shopping — clothes, sizes and returning items

title: Shopping — clothes, sizes & returning items
description: Learn to shop for clothes, ask about sizes, describe what you want, and return or exchange items.

content.explanation: These phrases are used in clothing shops around the world.

phrases.phrase: Do you have this in a different size?
It's a bit too tight / loose.
I'd like to return this, please.
I'd like to exchange this for a smaller size.

phrases.note: The item is broken/has a defect

content.explanation: Words used to describe size and how clothes fit.

items.phrase: a bit big / a bit small

content.explanation: Use 'too' and 'enough' to describe why something doesn't work.

structures.meaning: More than you want — a problem
Less than you need — a problem

content.instruction: Repeat clearly. Shopping conversations need confident, clear speech.

phrases.text: Do you have this in a medium, please?
Can I try this on? Where are the changing rooms?
It's a bit too tight — do you have a larger size?
I'd like to return this, please. It's faulty.
It fits perfectly! I'll take it.

content.question: The shirt you tried on is too small. What do you say to the assistant?
options: Do you have this in a larger size, please?
content.explanation: Do you have this in a larger size? = polite and natural when the item is too small. ✓

content.question: The trousers are very long — they touch the floor. What is the problem?
content.explanation: More length than you want = too long. They're too long. ✓

content.question: The jacket is small and uncomfortable on your shoulders. What is correct?
content.explanation: The jacket is smaller than you need = not big enough. ✓

tasks.title: Fill in too or not enough

content.sentence: This dress is ___ expensive — I can't afford it. And this one is ___ long — it reaches the floor. The blue one is nice but it's ___ small enough — I need a medium.
content.explanation: Can't afford → too expensive. Reaches floor → too long. Need medium → not big enough. ✓

content.sentence: I'd like to ___ this shirt, please — I bought it yesterday but it's ___. I have the ___ here. Could I ___ it for the same shirt in a medium?
content.explanation: return (give back) ✓, faulty (broken/damaged) ✓, receipt (proof of purchase) ✓, exchange (swap for different) ✓.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct return/exchange sentence.
content.correct_sentence: I'd like to exchange this for a larger size.
content.explanation: I'd like + to + exchange + this + for + a + adjective + noun.

content.instruction: Match each fit problem to the correct phrase.

pairs.prompt: The shirt is very tight across the chest.
The trousers fall down — they're very loose.
The dress is the right size but I don't like it on me.

content.context: Nisha is shopping for a dress for a wedding. She is helped by a shop assistant.

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Hello! Can I help you today?

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: Yes please! I'm looking for a dress for a wedding. Something elegant but not too formal.

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Of course! We have some beautiful new arrivals. What size are you?

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: Usually a small or medium. It depends on the style.

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: This green one is very popular. Would you like to try it on?

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: Yes! Can I try it in both small and medium? Where are the changing rooms?

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Right at the back on your left.

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: (After trying) The small is a bit too tight but the medium fits perfectly. But is it available in blue?

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Let me check. Yes — we have it in dark blue and light blue.

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: The dark blue is gorgeous! I'll take the medium in dark blue.

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Wonderful choice! Will you be paying by card or cash?

comprehension_questions.question: What is the occasion Nisha is shopping for?
Why doesn't the small dress work?
Which dress does Nisha decide to buy?

content.instruction: Choose the correctly formed sentence about a shopping problem.
content.wrong_sentence: This jacket is too much tight and the sleeves are not enough long.
options: This jacket is too much tight and the sleeves are not enough long.
This jacket is too tight and the sleeves are not long enough.
This jacket is too tight and the sleeves are not enough long.
This jacket is much too tight and the sleeves are long not enough.
content.explanation: too + adjective (not too much + adjective). not + adjective + enough (not enough comes after). ✓

content.scenario: You bought shoes last week but they are hurting your feet. The soles are coming off. You want to return them.
content.question: What do you say at the shop?
options: These shoes are bad. Give me money back.
I'd like to return these shoes please. I bought them last week and they're faulty — the soles are coming off. I have the receipt.
content.explanation: Complete return: I'd like to return + reason (faulty, soles coming off) + receipt. Professional. ✓

content.instruction: Speak for 65 seconds. Role play a complete clothing shop visit. Cover: asking for help, trying on, a problem with fit, requesting a different size, and buying.
content.prompt: You are shopping for a shirt for work. Play both the customer and the assistant.
content.example_response: Assistant: Hello! Can I help you today? Customer: Yes please — I'm looking for a formal shirt for work. Do you have something in white or light blue? Assistant: Of course! What size are you? Customer: I'm usually a medium. Could I try this white one on? Assistant: Certainly — the changing rooms are just over there. Customer: (After trying) Hmm — it's a bit too tight across the shoulders. Do you have this in a large? Assistant: Let me check. Yes, here you go. Customer: (Tries again) This is much better — it fits perfectly! How much is it? Assistant: It's 999 rupees. Customer: Great, I'll take it. Do you have any other colours in the large? Assistant: We have it in light blue too. Customer: Perfect — I'll take both! I'll pay by card.
key_phrases: It fits perfectly! I'll take it.

content.instruction: Speak for 50 seconds. You are returning a jacket you bought online. It arrived damaged. Role play the conversation at the returns desk.
content.prompt: Return a damaged jacket at a shop. Cover: greeting, reason for return, the problem, your preference (refund or exchange).
content.example_response: Good morning! I'd like to return this jacket, please. I ordered it online and it arrived two days ago but there's a problem — the zip is broken and there's a small tear near the pocket. I have the receipt here and the original packaging. The jacket itself is beautiful — I was really looking forward to wearing it. Could I exchange it for the same jacket in the same size if you have it in stock? If not, I'd like a full refund please. I paid by card — can the refund go back to my card? Thank you so much for your help.

content.sentence: The red dress is ___ expensive — it's 5000 rupees. The blue one is nice but it's ___ big enough — I need a smaller size. The green one fits well but it's ___ short — it's above my knee and I wanted something longer. The purple one is perfect — ___ tight, ___ loose. I'll take it!
content.explanation: too expensive ✓, not big enough ✓, too short ✓, not too tight ✓, not too loose ✓ (perfect fit).

content.instruction: Match each 'too' sentence to its 'not enough' equivalent.
Speak for 45 seconds. Describe the perfect outfit for a special occasion. Use clothing vocabulary, colours, and too/enough.
content.prompt: Describe what you would wear to a formal dinner. What would be perfect? What wouldn't work and why?
content.example_response: For a formal dinner I'd wear a dark navy suit — not too casual, formal enough for the occasion. The jacket should fit perfectly — not too tight, not too loose. I'd pair it with a crisp white shirt. Light colours are always elegant. The trousers should be the right length — not too long so they drag, not too short. I'd wear black shoes — classic and always appropriate. No bright colours — they're too casual for a formal dinner. A simple watch and a dark tie would complete the look. Smart but not overdressed!

what_you_learned: Shopping phrases: Can I try this on? / Do you have this in ___? / I'll take it.
Return/exchange: I'd like to return this. / It's faulty. / I have the receipt.
Size vocabulary: too tight/loose, fits perfectly, a bit big/small, suits me
Too + adjective: It's too expensive. (more than you want)
Not + adjective + enough: It's not big enough. (less than you need)
content.next_lesson_title: Eating out — ordering, complaints, and special requests

title: Eating out — ordering, complaints & special requests
description: Master restaurant conversations: detailed ordering, dietary requirements, making complaints politely, and complimenting food.

content.explanation: These words describe food restrictions or preferences — essential for eating out.

items.note: No meat, fish, dairy, or eggs
Cannot eat wheat, barley or rye
No milk, cheese, butter or cream
Simple way to say what you avoid

content.explanation: When food is wrong or there is a problem, complain politely — never rudely.

phrases.phrase: Excuse me, I think there might be a mistake.
I'm sorry to mention this, but ___.
This is a bit cold — could you warm it up?
I asked for no onions but there are onions in this.
I think this might be undercooked.

content.explanation: Complimenting the food makes the dining experience warmer and more enjoyable.

compliments.phrase: I've never tasted anything like this.

compliments.note: Formal — tell staff the chef did well

compliments.phrase: This is the best [dish] I've ever had!

describing_taste.note: Smooth, with a lot of cream

content.instruction: Repeat with natural restaurant tone — confident but never aggressive.

phrases.text: I'm vegetarian — does this dish contain meat?
Excuse me — I think there might be a mistake with my order.
I'm sorry to mention this but my food is cold.
This is absolutely delicious — my compliments to the chef!
Could I ask for the dish without onions, please?

content.question: You are vegetarian and the menu is unclear. What do you ask the waiter?
options: Is this vegetarian? Does it contain any meat or fish?
content.explanation: Is this vegetarian? + clarifying question = polite and complete. ✓

content.question: Your soup arrived but it is cold. What is the most polite complaint?
options: I'm sorry to mention this, but my soup is a bit cold — could you warm it up please?
This is bad — take it back.
content.explanation: I'm sorry to mention this + but + specific problem + polite request = professional complaint. ✓

content.question: You ordered chicken but received fish. What do you say?
options: Excuse me, I think there might be a mistake — I ordered the chicken, not the fish.
content.explanation: Excuse me + I think there might be a mistake + specific clarification = polite and clear. ✓

content.sentence: I'm ___ to nuts so I need to be careful. Does this dish ___ any nuts or nut oil? I'm also ___ so I don't eat meat or fish. Could you ___ me which dishes are suitable?
content.explanation: allergic to ✓, contain ✓, vegetarian ✓, tell me which ✓.

content.sentence: Excuse me, I'm ___ to mention this but I asked for no chilli in my curry and it's very spicy. I ___ this isn't what I ordered — I ordered the mild version. Could you ___ this, please? I'm sorry for the ___.
content.explanation: I'm sorry ✓, I think this isn't ✓, replace ✓, for the trouble ✓ (polite apology).

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a complete dietary request.
content.correct_sentence: Does this dish contain any dairy?
content.explanation: Does + this dish + contain + any + dietary item?

content.instruction: Match each food description word to its meaning.

pairs.answer: smooth, with cream or a soft texture

tasks.title: Dialogue — A restaurant with problems and compliments

content.context: Riya and her husband visit a restaurant. There are some issues but also great food.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Good evening! Are you ready to order?

dialogue.speaker: Riya
dialogue.line: Almost! I'm vegetarian — could you tell me which curries on the menu are meat-free?

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Of course. The dal makhani, paneer tikka, and the vegetable biryani are all vegetarian.

dialogue.speaker: Riya
dialogue.line: Perfect. I'll have the paneer tikka. Does it contain any cream? I'm dairy-free.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: It does have some cream in the sauce. We can make a dairy-free version for you.

dialogue.speaker: Riya
dialogue.line: That would be wonderful, thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Husband
dialogue.line: I'll have the lamb biryani, please.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: (Later) Here are your dishes. Enjoy!

dialogue.speaker: Riya
dialogue.line: Excuse me — I'm sorry to mention this but my paneer is quite cold. Could you warm it up please?

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: I'm so sorry! I'll get that sorted immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Riya
dialogue.line: (After warming) Oh wow — this is absolutely delicious! The spices are perfectly balanced.

dialogue.speaker: Husband
dialogue.line: My biryani is amazing too. My compliments to the chef!

comprehension_questions.question: Why does Riya ask about the cream?
What is wrong with Riya's food when it arrives?
How does Riya feel about the food after it is warmed?

content.instruction: Which complaint is worded rudely and should NOT be used?
content.wrong_sentence: Oi! This food is cold and disgusting. What kind of restaurant is this?
options: Oi! This food is cold and disgusting. What kind of restaurant is this?
Excuse me — I'm sorry to mention this but my food arrived a bit cold.
I'm sorry to bother you, but I think there might be a mistake with my order.
Could you check this for me please? I think it's slightly undercooked.
content.explanation: Rude, aggressive, insulting. In English restaurants, complaints must be calm, polite, and specific. ✓

content.scenario: You are eating at a restaurant. The waiter brings you fish curry but you ordered vegetable curry and you are vegetarian.
content.question: What is the best way to handle this?
options: Excuse me — I think there might be a mistake. I ordered the vegetable curry. I'm actually vegetarian so I can't eat this. Could you please replace it?
content.explanation: Polite explanation + clear reason (vegetarian) + specific request (replace) = perfect complaint. ✓

tasks.title: Speaking — Full restaurant with dietary needs

content.instruction: Speak for 65 seconds. Role play a complete restaurant experience. You have a dietary restriction (choose one). A problem occurs with your order but the food eventually impresses you.
content.prompt: Have a full restaurant conversation: greet, order with dietary needs, a complaint, a compliment.
content.example_response: Good evening! Thank you. Could I see the menu please? I'm gluten-free — could you tell me which dishes don't contain wheat or flour? The grilled fish with vegetables sounds perfect — does it come with any sauce that might contain flour? No flour — wonderful! I'll have that, and could I have the salad as a starter please? (After starter) Excuse me — I'm sorry to mention this but there are croutons on my salad and I'm gluten-free. Could you bring a fresh one without croutons? I appreciate it, thank you. (After main) Oh my goodness — this fish is absolutely divine. It's so fresh and the seasoning is perfect. Could I ask what herbs are used? This is genuinely the best fish I've had in months. My compliments to the chef — truly exceptional!
key_phrases: I'm sorry to mention this but ___

content.instruction: Speak for 50 seconds. Recommend a restaurant you love to a friend. Describe the food using rich taste vocabulary.
content.prompt: Tell me about your favourite restaurant. What do you love about it? Describe the food.
content.example_response: There's a small South Indian restaurant near my office that I absolutely love. It's nothing fancy from the outside but the food is extraordinary. Their sambar is incredibly rich and aromatic — you can smell it the moment you walk in! The idlis are perfectly soft and fluffy, and the chutneys are so well-seasoned and tangy. They have a prawn curry that is creamy and full of flavour — it's the best I've ever tasted anywhere. The service is warm and quick and the prices are very reasonable — not too expensive at all. I've taken many friends there and everyone loves it. I'd highly recommend going on a weekday — it gets very crowded on weekends!

content.sentence: I'm ___ to any nuts. Before I order — ___ this dish contain nuts or nut oil? Also I ___ eat spicy food — is this mild? Great. I'll ___ the vegetable korma without nuts. ___, I'm sorry to mention this — my food arrived cold. Could you ___ it please?
content.explanation: allergic to ✓, Does contain ✓, can't eat ✓, have (order) ✓, Excuse me ✓, warm it up ✓.

content.instruction: Match each restaurant problem to the most appropriate polite complaint.

pairs.answer: Excuse me, I think there might be a mistake — I ordered the chicken not the fish.
I'm sorry to mention this but my food is a bit cold — could you warm it up?

pairs.prompt: You asked for no onions but they're there
pairs.answer: I asked for no onions but there are some in this — could you replace it?
I think this might be slightly undercooked — could someone check please?
This is absolutely delicious — my compliments to the chef!

content.instruction: Speak for 45 seconds. Describe the best meal you ever ate. Use taste vocabulary: rich, creamy, tangy, aromatic, well-seasoned, delicious.
content.prompt: What is the best meal you have ever eaten? Describe it in detail.
content.example_response: The best meal I ever ate was at a small coastal restaurant in Kerala about two years ago. I had a whole grilled fish with rice and a coconut curry. The fish was incredibly fresh — you could taste the sea in every bite. The coconut curry was rich and creamy with a slightly tangy undertone from the tamarind. The whole dish was beautifully aromatic — the curry leaves and mustard seeds created this wonderful smell. The rice was perfectly cooked — light and fluffy. Everything was so well-seasoned — not too spicy, not too mild — just absolutely perfect. I still think about that meal!
key_phrases: rich / creamy / tangy / aromatic / well-seasoned / fresh / delicious / absolutely

what_you_learned: Dietary words: vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, allergic to
Questions: Does this contain ___? Is this vegetarian/gluten-free?
Polite complaints: Excuse me, I think there might be a mistake. / I'm sorry to mention this but ___.
Compliments: This is absolutely delicious! / My compliments to the chef!
Taste vocabulary: rich, creamy, tangy, aromatic, well-seasoned
content.next_lesson_title: Health and lifestyle — healthy habits, advice

title: Health & lifestyle — habits, advice & should
description: Talk about healthy and unhealthy habits, give health advice using should/shouldn't, and discuss lifestyle choices.

content.explanation: Vocabulary for discussing lifestyle and health habits.

healthy.example: I try to exercise at least three times a week.
She eats vegetables and protein every day.
I drink eight glasses of water a day.
I try to sleep eight hours every night.
He meditates every morning to manage stress.

unhealthy.example: I often skip breakfast — I know it's bad!
She stays up until midnight every night.
He eats fast food almost every day.
I sit at my desk for 8 hours without a break.
I drink five cups of coffee a day.

content.explanation: Should = good idea / recommendation. Shouldn't = bad idea / not recommended.

advice_examples.advice: You should try to exercise more.
You should go to bed earlier.
You shouldn't eat so much salt.

content.explanation: Phrases to describe how you live your daily life.

items.note: You make an effort — I try to exercise.
Your usual habit — I tend to skip lunch.
Recent effort — I've been trying to sleep earlier.

content.instruction: Repeat these health advice phrases with a warm, caring tone.

phrases.text: You should try to get more sleep — it makes a huge difference.
I try to exercise three times a week but I'm not always successful.
You shouldn't skip meals — it's really bad for your energy levels.
Have you tried meditating? It might help with your stress.
I could definitely be better at drinking enough water.

content.question: Your friend is always tired. What advice do you give?
options: You should stay up late every night.
You should try to go to bed earlier and get at least eight hours.
content.explanation: Positive health advice → should. You should go to bed earlier + practical detail. ✓

content.question: Your colleague is very stressed. Which is the GENTLEST piece of advice?
options: You could try taking short breaks during the day — it might help.
content.explanation: Could try = very gentle suggestion. Might help = tentative — least pressure on the person. ✓

content.question: You eat vegetables sometimes but not every day. How do you describe this honestly?
options: I try to eat vegetables but I could be better at it.
content.explanation: I try to = making effort. I could be better = honest admission you could improve. ✓

content.sentence: You ___ drink so much coffee — it's affecting your sleep. You ___ try herbal tea instead. You ___ also set a bedtime alarm — it might help you sleep earlier. And you really ___ skip breakfast — it gives you energy for the whole day.
content.explanation: Too much coffee = bad → shouldn't. Suggestion → could. Skip breakfast = bad → shouldn't.

content.sentence: I ___ to exercise every morning but I'm not always successful. I ___ to skip lunch when work gets busy — I know it's bad. I've been ___ to drink more water recently. I could ___ better at managing stress.
content.explanation: I try to (effort) ✓, I tend to (usual habit) ✓, I've been trying to (recent effort) ✓, I could be better ✓.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a complete health advice sentence.
content.correct_sentence: I think you should drink at least six glasses of water more.
content.hint: I think + you + should + drink + at least + amount
content.correct_sentence_v2: I think you should drink more water — at least six glasses.
content.explanation: I think (softens advice) + you should + base verb + recommendation.

content.instruction: Match each unhealthy habit to the most appropriate piece of advice.

pairs.answer: You should try to walk for 30 minutes a day.

pairs.prompt: I stay up until 2am every night.
pairs.answer: You should try to go to bed before midnight.
You shouldn't skip breakfast — it affects your energy.

pairs.prompt: I sit at my desk for 8 hours non-stop.
pairs.answer: You could try taking a short break every hour.

pairs.prompt: I drink 6 cups of coffee a day.
pairs.answer: You should cut down on coffee — it's affecting your sleep.

content.context: Friends Sonal and Rohan discuss their health habits over lunch.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.line: Rohan, you look a bit tired. Are you okay?

dialogue.speaker: Rohan
dialogue.line: I'm exhausted honestly. I've been staying up very late to finish my project.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.line: You really shouldn't do that — sleep deprivation is really harmful.

dialogue.speaker: Rohan
dialogue.line: I know, I know. I just can't switch off at night.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.line: Have you tried switching off your phone an hour before bed? It really helps.

dialogue.speaker: Rohan
dialogue.line: That's a good idea. I tend to scroll through my phone until midnight.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.line: You should try it tonight. Also — are you eating well?

dialogue.speaker: Rohan
dialogue.line: Not really. I skip breakfast and I tend to eat junk food when I'm stressed.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.line: You shouldn't skip breakfast! You could try meal prepping on Sundays — it makes weekdays so much easier.

dialogue.speaker: Rohan
dialogue.line: You're right. I'm really not looking after myself properly.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.line: How about we go for a walk after work? Even 20 minutes makes a difference.

dialogue.speaker: Rohan
dialogue.line: That sounds great. I should definitely move more — I sit at my desk all day.

comprehension_questions.question: Why is Rohan exhausted?
options: He has been staying up late

comprehension_questions.question: What does Sonal suggest to help Rohan sleep better?
options: Switch off the phone an hour before bed

comprehension_questions.question: What do they decide to do after work?

tasks.title: Find the mistake in health advice

content.instruction: Choose the sentence with correct grammar.
content.wrong_sentence: You should to exercise more and you shouldn't to eat so much sugar.
options: You should to exercise more and you shouldn't to eat so much sugar.
You should exercise more and you shouldn't eat so much sugar.
You should exercising more and you shouldn't eating so much sugar.
You should exercises more and you shouldn't eats so much sugar.
content.explanation: Should/shouldn't + BASE verb only. No 'to', no -ing, no -s. should exercise ✓, shouldn't eat ✓.

content.scenario: Your friend says: 'I have terrible headaches every afternoon.' What advice do you give?
content.question: Which is the most helpful and natural response?
options: You should drink more water — dehydration causes headaches. You should also try taking a short break from your screen every hour. Have you seen a doctor?
content.explanation: Multiple practical suggestions + should + question showing care. Complete helpful response. ✓

content.instruction: Speak for 65 seconds. Describe your current health habits honestly — what you do well and what you could improve. Use: I try to / I tend to / I should / I shouldn't.
content.prompt: Tell me about your lifestyle. What healthy habits do you have? What unhealthy habits do you want to change?
content.example_response: Honestly my lifestyle is a mix of good and bad habits! I try to exercise three times a week — I usually go for a walk or do yoga. I'm quite good about eating vegetables and drinking enough water. However, I tend to skip breakfast when I'm in a rush — I know I shouldn't do that. I stay up too late most nights — I should go to bed before 11 but I usually don't. I've been trying to reduce my coffee intake recently because I drink about four cups a day and it affects my sleep. I sit at my desk for long periods without breaks — I could be much better about standing up and moving. And I could be better at managing stress — I tend to worry too much. I'm working on it though!

content.instruction: Speak for 55 seconds. A friend describes these problems: always tired, back pain, stressed, gaining weight. Give them specific advice for each problem.
content.prompt: Your friend is always tired, has back pain, is very stressed, and has been gaining weight. Give them helpful, caring advice.
content.example_response: For the tiredness — you should really look at your sleep routine. You shouldn't stay up late scrolling on your phone. Try to go to bed at the same time every night. For your back pain — you should see a physiotherapist. You could also try doing some simple stretches in the morning and you shouldn't sit for more than an hour without standing up. For stress — have you tried meditation or breathing exercises? Even five minutes a day could make a difference. You could also try writing down your worries in a journal. For the weight — you shouldn't skip meals because that actually makes weight gain worse. You should try to eat smaller, regular meals and maybe walk for 30 minutes a day. None of this needs to be extreme — small changes make a big difference!

content.sentence: If you want to be healthier, here is my advice. First, you ___ drink at least 8 glasses of water a day. You ___ drink too much caffeine — it disrupts sleep. You ___ try to exercise at least three times a week — even a 20-minute walk helps. You ___ sit for long periods without moving. Finally, you ___ try to sleep at the same time every night. It ___ sound simple but these habits make an enormous difference!
content.explanation: Positive recommendations → should. Warnings → shouldn't. It might sound = tentative. ✓

tasks.title: Speaking — Compare your habits now vs before

content.instruction: Speak for 50 seconds. Compare your current health habits to how you lived 5 years ago. Use used to, didn't use to, now I, and comparatives.
content.prompt: How are your health habits different from five years ago? Better or worse?
content.example_response: My health habits are definitely better now than they were five years ago! I used to skip breakfast every day and eat junk food constantly. Now I try to eat properly. I used to never exercise — now I go to the gym twice a week and walk every morning. I used to stay up until 2am — now I go to bed by 11. However I drink more coffee now than I used to — that's worse! And I'm more stressed now than before because my job is more demanding. Overall though, I look after myself much better than I did. I think I'm more aware of my health now — I couldn't care less before!
key_phrases: I used to ___ but now I ___

content.instruction: Match each sentence to how strong the advice is.

pairs.prompt: You shouldn't eat so much sugar.
Have you tried drinking less coffee?
Why don't you go to bed earlier?

what_you_learned: Healthy habits: exercise regularly, eat balanced diet, drink water, get enough sleep, manage stress
Unhealthy habits: skip meals, stay up late, eat junk food, sit too long, drink too much coffee
Should + base verb = recommendation / Shouldn't + base verb = warning
Softer advice: could try, might help, have you tried, why don't you
Lifestyle phrases: I try to / I tend to / I've been trying to / I could be better at
content.next_lesson_title: Directions — asking and giving directions in detail
